mnemonics.title: T-Distribution Properties
mnemonics.content: T-SN: TALLER TAILS THAN NORMAL - As df increases, T approaches Standard Normal (SN)

mnemonics.title: Degrees of Freedom Formula
mnemonics.content: DF = N - 1: Degrees of Freedom equals Sample size minus One for one-sample tests

mnemonics.title: T-Statistic Formula
mnemonics.content: TOP = (Sample Mean - Population Mean): BOTTOM = Standard Error = s/√n. So t = TOP/BOTTOM

acronyms.expansion: Two-Tail TEST: Tests if means are different in either direction (greater or less)
ONE-Tail Alternative TEST: Tests if mean is greater OR less (not both)
Degrees of Freedom: Number of independent values that can vary in a calculation

visualTips.visualization: Draw both curves on same axes. T-curve is wider with higher peaks at tails. As you increase sample size (draw more curves), the t-curve looks more like the normal curve.
Imagine a number line. For α=0.05 two-tailed test, shade the outer 2.5% on both ends. The t-statistic falling in shaded region means reject null.
For paired, draw boxes connected by lines showing same subjects measured twice. For independent, draw two separate boxes showing different groups.
Think of t as 'signal-to-noise ratio': difference between means (signal) divided by variability (noise). Larger t = stronger evidence against null.

analogies.analogy: Imagine throwing darts at a target. With small samples (fewer throws), your aim is less certain - your misses spread further out. That's like heavier tails. With more throws (larger df), you get better and your misses cluster closer - like normal distribution.
Think of estimating mean from n numbers. You can freely choose n-1 numbers, but the last one is fixed to get your chosen mean. That's why df = n-1.

Using a regular ruler vs a flexible measuring tape. Z-test is like a rigid ruler (assumes you know exact spread). T-test is like a flexible tape that accounts for uncertainty when you don't know the exact spread from small samples.
Before-and-after weight loss measurements on same 10 people = paired. Comparing weight loss between 10 people in diet group vs 10 different people in control group = independent samples.
